message:
feat(demo_showcase): headless check mode and panic recovery hook

Headless testing improvements:
- Add --headless-check <NAME> flag for running specific headless checks
  (layout, config, palette, hitgrid, logs) without full smoke test
- Validates check names against allowed values with clear error messages
- Enables targeted debugging of individual rendering subsystems

Terminal safety improvements:
- Add install_panic_hook() that captures panics and attempts terminal
  restoration before re-throwing
- Writes ANSI reset sequences directly to stderr to restore cursor,
  attributes, and alternate screen state
- Provides user-friendly recovery instructions ("run: reset" or
  "stty sane && clear") for cases where automatic recovery fails
- Chains to original panic handler to preserve standard panic output

These changes improve debugging workflows for headless environments and
prevent terminal corruption when the application panics during TUI mode.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-1al","title":"demo_showcase UI: overlay system (modals + glass + focus capture)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Overlay System (Modals + Glass + Focus Capture)

## Purpose

The demo app needs multiple overlays that all share the same core behavior:

- semi-transparent “glass” panels layered over the main UI
- dimmed / tinted backdrop
- animated enter/exit (subtle fade / slide)
- input capture (overlay takes focus; underlying UI stops receiving events)
- hit testing for clickable controls inside overlays

This bead builds a reusable overlay foundation so **Help**, **Command Palette**, and **Tour** don’t each reinvent modal behavior.

## Core Requirements

### Rendering

- Overlays render in the final render pass (highest z-order).
- Backdrop uses `push_opacity()` + translucent `fill_rect()` so alpha blending is obvious.
- Overlay body uses:
  - rounded/double border styles
  - soft gradients / subtle noise patterns (procedural) to look “premium”
  - clipped internal scrolling content (scissor stack)

### Input / Focus

- Overlay manager owns:
  - `active: Option<Overlay>` (or a small stack if needed)
  - `focus: OverlayFocus` (which control is focused)
- When an overlay is active:
  - keyboard shortcuts are handled by overlay first
  - mouse hit testing only targets overlay regions
  - optional “click outside to dismiss” behavior

### HitGrid integration

- Overlay rendering must also register hit areas (`Renderer::register_hit_area`).
- Use `Renderer::push_hit_scissor` around overlay internals so hit areas respect clipping.

## Suggested Structure (demo-only)

Inside `src/bin/demo_showcase.rs`:

- `enum Overlay { Help(HelpState), Palette(PaletteState), Tour(TourState) }`
- `struct OverlayManager { active: Option<Overlay>, anim: OverlayAnim, ... }`
- `fn render_overlays(&mut self, ctx: &mut RenderCtx, ...)` called from the render pass bead.

## Acceptance Notes

We consider this “done” when:

- The overlay visuals clearly demonstrate alpha blending + opacity stack (no “flat” look).
- Focus is trapped inside overlays and underlying UI does not react.
- Overlay hit targets work and do not leak outside clipped regions.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:

## Purpose

Even though `Renderer` and `Terminal` try to restore state on Drop, a panic can still leave the user’s terminal in a bad state (especially if aborting).

This bead adds belt-and-suspenders cleanup so the demo is “safe to run”.

## Approach

- Install a panic hook that:
  - attempts best-effort cleanup
  - prints a helpful message (`reset` / `stty sane`)

Important:

- Keep it minimal and avoid introducing dependencies.
- Do not swallow panic info; chain to the original hook.

## Scope

 {"id":"bd-1c6","title":"[EPIC] Expanded Examples Suite","description":"# Epic: Expanded Examples Suite

## Background & Motivation

OpenTUI currently has only two examples:
1. `hello.rs` - Basic buffer creation and text drawing (doesn't even render to terminal)
2. `editor.rs` - Full interactive editor demo

There's a significant gap between these. Users need:
- Simple, focused examples showing one feature at a time
- Progressive complexity to learn the library
- Real-world patterns they can copy into their projects
- Examples demonstrating all major features

## Goals

1. Provide examples covering all major OpenTUI features
2. Order examples by complexity (beginner → advanced)
3. Each example should be self-contained and focused on one concept
4. Include inline comments explaining key patterns
5. Examples should be runnable and visually interesting

## Proposed Example Structure

```
examples/
├── 01_hello_terminal.rs     # Basic terminal setup + text output
├── 02_colors.rs             # RGBA colors, hex, HSV, blending
├── 03_styles.rs             # Bold, italic, underline, inverse
├── 04_drawing.rs            # Boxes, lines, fill_rect
├── 05_scissoring.rs         # Scissor clipping demonstration
├── 06_opacity.rs            # Opacity stacks and transparency
├── 07_input.rs              # Keyboard and mouse input handling
├── 08_animation.rs          # Simple animation loop
├── 09_text_buffer.rs        # TextBuffer and styled segments
├── 10_edit_buffer.rs        # Editable text with cursor
├── 11_highlighting.rs       # Syntax highlighting (after highlight impl)
├── 12_panels.rs             # Multiple panels/windows pattern
├── 13_menu.rs               # Dropdown/popup menu pattern
├── 14_progress.rs           # Progress bars and spinners
├── 15_dashboard.rs          # Complex multi-widget dashboard
└── editor.rs                # (Existing) Full editor demo
```

## Design Principles

- **Progressive disclosure** - Start simple, build complexity
- **One concept per example** - Don't overwhelm with features
- **Copy-paste friendly** - Code should work with minimal modification
- **Visual appeal** - Make examples look good so users are inspired
- **Commented thoroughly** - Explain the "why" not just the "what"

## Non-Goals

- Complete application examples (that's what the editor demo is for)
- Platform-specific examples
- Integration with external crates (keep dependencies minimal)

## Success Criteria

- [ ] At least 10 new example files
- [ ] Examples build and run without errors
- [ ] README or doc comments explain what each example demonstrates
- [ ] New user can understand OpenTUI by reading examples in order
- [ ] Examples showcase features not obvious from API docs

## Dependencies

Most examples can be created immediately. The highlighting example (11) depends on the syntax highlighting epic completion.

## Estimated Scope

~1500-2000 LOC across all examples.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T01:09:23.805421096Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T17:55:13.555654383Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T17:55:13.555508939Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["epic","examples"]}
 {"id":"bd-1c6.1","title":"Example: Basic terminal setup and rendering","description":"# Example: Basic Terminal Setup and Rendering

## Purpose

This is the true "hello world" - demonstrating the minimum code to:
1. Create a Renderer
2. Draw something to the buffer
3. Present it to the terminal
4. Handle cleanup

The existing `hello.rs` doesn't actually render to terminal - it just creates a buffer and prints dimensions. This example fills that gap.

## Code Structure

```rust
//! Example 01: Basic Terminal Rendering
//! 
//! This example demonstrates the fundamental OpenTUI rendering loop:
//! 1. Create a Renderer (enters alternate screen, hides cursor)
//! 2. Get the back buffer and draw to it
//! 3. Present the frame (writes to terminal)
//! 4. Wait for user input to exit
//! 5. Renderer drops and restores terminal automatically

use opentui::{Renderer, Rgba, Style};
use std::io::{self, Read};

fn main() -> io::Result<()> {
    // Create renderer with terminal dimensions
    // This automatically:
    // - Enters alternate screen (saves current terminal content)
    // - Hides the cursor
    // - Enables raw mode (direct key input)
    let mut renderer = Renderer::new(80, 24)?;
    
    // Clear the buffer with a dark background
    let buffer = renderer.buffer();
    buffer.clear(Rgba::from_hex("#1a1a2e").unwrap());
    
    // Draw centered text
    let title = "Welcome to OpenTUI!";
    let x = (80 - title.len() as u32) / 2;
    buffer.draw_text(x, 10, title, Style::fg(Rgba::from_hex("#00ff88").unwrap()).with_bold());
    
    let subtitle = "Press any key to exit...";
    let x = (80 - subtitle.len() as u32) / 2;
    buffer.draw_text(x, 12, subtitle, Style::fg(Rgba::from_hex("#888888").unwrap()));
    
    // Present the frame (diff-based, efficient)
    renderer.present()?;
    
    // Wait for any key
    let _ = io::stdin().read(&mut [0u8]);
    
    // Renderer::drop() automatically:
    // - Exits alternate screen (restores previous content)
    // - Shows the cursor
    // - Disables raw mode
    
    Ok(())
}
```

## Key Concepts Demonstrated

1. **Renderer lifecycle** - Create → draw → present → drop
2. **Alternate screen** - Terminal content preserved
3. **Automatic cleanup** - RAII pattern with Drop trait
4. **Basic drawing** - `clear()` and `draw_text()`
5. **Color creation** - `Rgba::from_hex()`
6. **Style application** - `Style::fg()` and `.with_bold()`

## Testing

- Run example: `cargo run --example 01_hello_terminal`
- Verify text appears centered on dark background
- Verify pressing a key exits cleanly
- Verify terminal is restored after exit

## File to Create

- `examples/01_hello_terminal.rs`

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Example compiles and runs
- [ ] Output is visually appealing
- [ ] Terminal restores properly on exit
- [ ] Code has thorough comments
- [ ] Demonstrates minimum viable OpenTUI usage","notes":"## Testing Requirements

Per bd-1c6.11 (Examples Validation Test Suite):
- Example MUST compile with `cargo build --example 01_hello_terminal`
- Run with: `cargo run --example 01_hello_terminal`
- Verify terminal restores correctly on exit (no corruption)
- Test on both light and dark terminal backgrounds

## User Experience

This is the FIRST example users will see. It must:
- Work flawlessly on first try
- Have clear, educational comments
- Demonstrate OpenTUI's "just works" philosophy
- Leave the terminal in pristine condition on exit","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-25T01:09:42.262261577Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-25T12:41:42.829409444Z","closed_at":"2026-01-25T12:41:42.829391049Z","close_reason":"Added 01_hello_terminal example with renderer flow and README entry","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["beginner","examples"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1c6.1","depends_on_id":"bd-1c6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:52:23Z","created_by":"import"}]}

## Implementation Structure

```rust
struct Dashboard {
    panels: Vec<Panel>,
    focused: usize,
    
    // Panel-specific state
    sidebar_selection: usize,
    event_log: Vec<LogEntry>,
}

impl Dashboard {
    fn handle_input(&mut self, event: &Event) {
        match event {
            Event::Key(key) if key.code == KeyCode::Tab => {
                self.focused = (self.focused + 1) % self.panels.len();
            }
            _ => {
                // Delegate to focused panel
                self.panels[self.focused].handle_input(event);
            }
        }
    }
    
    fn render(&self, buffer: &mut OptimizedBuffer) {
        for (i, panel) in self.panels.iter().enumerate() {
            panel.render(buffer, i == self.focused);
        }
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-2el","title":"demo_showcase runtime: headless smoke mode (no TTY, render-to-memory, exit 0)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Headless Smoke Mode (No TTY, Render-to-Memory, Exit 0)

## Purpose

We want a CI-friendly way to validate that the demo binary:

- compiles
- can construct its app state
- can execute its render pipeline

…without requiring a real terminal.

This enables deterministic tests, fast iteration, and regression snapshots.

## Behavior

Primary flag:

- --headless-smoke

When enabled:

- do not enter alt screen
- do not enable raw mode
- do not attempt mouse tracking
- build App + DemoContent
- render into an in-memory OptimizedBuffer
- exercise diff computation between frames (via BufferDiff::compute) to prove the diff path
- exit 0 if all frames render without panic

## Frame Count / Termination Rules

There are two headless execution modes:

### A) Plain headless smoke (no tour)

- default: run a small fixed N frames (fast in CI)
- if `--max-frames <N>` is provided, use that as the frame count

### B) Headless tour run (`--tour`)

- default: run until the tour script completes, then exit 0
  - (implementation can be "fast" by advancing time deterministically without sleeping)
- if `--max-frames <N>` is provided, treat it as a hard safety bound:
  - exit 0 only if tour completed within N frames
  - otherwise exit non-zero with a clear error

This avoids forcing tests to guess the number of frames required to finish a tour.

## Size + Capability Control (For Matrix Testing)

- `--headless-size <WxH>` forces the headless buffer size (default 80x24)
- `--cap-preset <name>` defines the effective capabilities used by gating and rendering decisions

## Tour Integration

- If --tour is set, headless mode executes the tour script deterministically.
- Tour step markers must be included in the JSON dump.

## Output Contract (For Tests)

Headless mode must print a stable, parseable summary line to stdout so integration tests can assert success without guessing:

- HEADLESS_SMOKE_OK frames=<N> last_dirty_cells=<K>

Structured dump (recommended for deeper tests):

- --headless-dump-json

When enabled, print JSON to stdout containing (minimum):

- config summary (theme, fps cap, seed, flags)
- effective capabilities used (after cap preset / gating)
- headless size used (WxH)
- selected layout mode (full vs compact)
- warnings list (e.g., "no_truecolor", "no_mouse", "no_hyperlinks")
- per-frame stats (dirty cell count, dt)
- sentinel markers proving panels rendered (top bar title, panel headers, current section)
- tour step markers (if tour enabled)

Avoid dumping the full buffer unless explicitly requested (too large for CI logs).

## Implementation Notes

This likely requires the demo’s render pipeline to be factored into a pure function:

- fn render_frame(app, layout, buffer, pool, links, now)

Renderer I/O should be a thin wrapper around that.

## Acceptance Notes

- `cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --headless-smoke` works in non-TTY CI environments.
- Results are deterministic across runs.
- Output contains HEADLESS_SMOKE_OK on success.
- JSON schema includes the fields required by `bd-2649` and `bd-2bnv`.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- `cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --headless-smoke` works in non-TTY CI environments and exits 0.
- Success output includes `HEADLESS_SMOKE_OK` with stable key/value fields.
- With `--headless-dump-json`, stdout includes valid JSON containing: effective caps, headless size, layout mode, warnings, and sentinel markers.
- `--headless-size` and `--cap-preset` change the effective caps/size reflected in JSON (enabling matrix snapshots).
- With `--tour`, headless execution completes the tour and exits 0 by default; `--max-frames` can bound runtime and fails clearly if too low.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:26:05.942661331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T03:19:33.194009288Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T03:19:33.193939809Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","runtime","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2el","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:26:05.964184936Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2el","depends_on_id":"bd-2iv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:12.868058851Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2el","depends_on_id":"bd-35g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:14.567529855Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2el","depends_on_id":"bd-3lio","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:28:50.268631831Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2el","depends_on_id":"bd-hc0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:14.001649143Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-2el","depends_on_id":"bd-po1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:13.424913256Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

 {"id":"bd-3l0","title":"demo_showcase spec: keybindings + interaction model (keyboard/mouse/focus)","description":"# demo_showcase Spec — Keybindings + Interaction Model

## Philosophy

- **Discoverable**: status bar + help overlay always tell you what to do.
- **Low cognitive load**: a small set of global chords; panel-local keys only when focused.
- **Mouse is first-class**: click to focus; scroll to scroll; clickable buttons prove HitGrid.

## Global Keybindings (Always Active)

- `Ctrl+Q` — Quit
- `F1` — Help overlay (toggle)
- `Ctrl+P` — Command palette (toggle)
- `Ctrl+T` — Tour mode (toggle / start from step 1)
- `Ctrl+D` — Toggle OpenTUI debug overlay (fps/cells/memory)
- `Tab` / `Shift+Tab` — Cycle focused panel
- `Ctrl+R` — Force full redraw (`renderer.invalidate()`)

## Panel Focus Model

There is always exactly one **focused panel**:

- Focus is drawn (border highlight + subtle glow)
- Keyboard events route primarily to the focused panel
- Mouse click inside a panel sets focus

Panels:
- Sidebar
- Editor
- Preview
- Logs

## Mouse Interaction Model (Must Exercise HitGrid)

- Click on sidebar items selects section
- Click on top bar “buttons” toggles overlays (Help / Palette / Tour)
- Scroll wheel over sidebar/logs scrolls that panel
- Click on hyperlink-like rows shows visual pressed feedback (even though link open is handled by terminal)

Implementation hint: register hit areas for:

- Each sidebar row
- Each top-bar button
- Selected preview “tabs”

## Input Events to Explicitly Surface

The app should *visibly react* to:

- Focus gained/lost events:
  - When focus lost: pause animations, show “PAUSED (focus lost)” badge
  - When focus gained: resume
- Bracketed paste:
  - Paste into editor inserts text and shows toast “Pasted N chars”
- Resize:
  - Layout recompute + toast “Resized to WxH”

## Editor Keybindings (When Editor Focused)

Keep it close to existing `examples/editor.rs`:

- Arrow keys: move cursor
- `Ctrl+Left/Right`: word navigation
- `Home/End`: line start/end
- `PageUp/PageDown`: scroll
- `Ctrl+Z` / `Ctrl+Y`: undo/redo
- `Ctrl+W`: cycle wrap mode (None → Word → Char)
- `Ctrl+L`: toggle line numbers

## Overlay Keybindings

- Help overlay:
  - `Esc` / `F1` to close
- Command palette:
  - `Esc` close
  - `Enter` activate selected action
  - Up/Down navigate list
- Tour:
  - `Enter` / `Space` next step
  - `Backspace` previous step
  - `Esc` exit tour","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:
- [ ] Global keybindings defined and minimal
- [ ] Focus model defined and consistent
- [ ] Mouse behaviors defined (click, scroll, hit testing targets)

- [ ] Bracketed paste behavior defined (insert + toast)
- [ ] Editor keys defined (undo/redo, wrap, line numbers, navigation)
- [ ] Overlay keys defined (help/palette/tour)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:05:42.145358418Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T00:49:13.775553150Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T00:49:13.775424420Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","design","input"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3l0","depends_on_id":"bd-2jc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:05:42.168791836Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3l0","depends_on_id":"bd-2mu","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:07:55.958980961Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3lio","title":"demo_showcase runtime: hard frame budget (--max-frames)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Hard Frame Budget (`--max-frames`)

## Purpose

We need a deterministic, mode-independent way to bound runtime so we can:

- run PTY E2E tests without hanging
- run quick headless smoke checks with consistent cost
- record short, repeatable demo clips

This bead implements the *runtime semantics* of a hard frame budget.

## Behavior

- Flag: `--max-frames <N>`
- After the app has **presented N frames**, it exits 0.

Counting rules:

- Count a “frame” after a successful `present()` (or after a successful headless render+diff step).
- The counter applies in **all modes**:
  - interactive normal
  - interactive tour
  - headless smoke
  - headless tour

Exit contract:

- Print a stable summary line to stdout:
  - `EXIT_OK reason=max_frames frames=<N> last_dirty_cells=<K>`

## Design Notes

- This is intentionally separate from `--exit-after-tour`:
  - `--exit-after-tour` is a tour completion feature.
  - `--max-frames` is a generic safety bound and developer tool.

## Downstream Consumers

- PTY E2E harness (`bd-61g0`) uses `--max-frames` as a hard safety bound.
- Headless smoke/snapshots (`bd-2el`, `bd-1n67`, `bd-2649`, `bd-bqd1`, `bd-2bnv`) use `--max-frames` to keep runs fast and deterministic.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- `--max-frames <N>` exits 0 after presenting N frames in interactive mode.
- `--max-frames <N>` also bounds headless runs consistently (smoke and tour), counting frames deterministically.
- A stable exit summary line is printed (`EXIT_OK reason=max_frames ...`) and includes `last_dirty_cells`.
- PTY E2E tests can use `--max-frames` as a hang-proof safety bound.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:28:16.243660372Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:27:36.270704114Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:27:36.270561729Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","runtime","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-3lio","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:28:16.259983646Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3lio","depends_on_id":"bd-2iv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:28:16.267009792Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-3lio","depends_on_id":"bd-z1b","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T23:28:16.264016443Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-3o0","title":"demo_showcase runtime: tour mode driver (script executor + deterministic timing)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Tour Mode Driver (Script Executor + Deterministic Timing)

## Purpose

Tour mode is how we get:

- unattended demos (screen recordings)
- deterministic behavior for tests/smoke runs
- a guided narrative that proves features in a clear order

This bead implements the runtime that executes the tour script defined in `bd-1gy`.

## Behaviors

- CLI flag --tour starts tour immediately.
- Tour runs a fixed sequence of steps:
  - changes focus/panels
  - triggers overlays (command palette/help)
  - scrolls lists/logs
  - types/pastes into editor
  - triggers preview animations / errors / recovery

Determinism:

- no randomness
- timing driven by a monotonic clock + fixed durations
- any synthetic inputs are generated by the tour driver

Automatic termination (for recordings/E2E):

- If `--exit-after-tour` is enabled (see `bd-3vjm` / `bd-2iv`), the app should exit 0 when the final tour step completes.
- `--max-frames` acts as a hard safety bound; it should never be required for a normal tour, but it prevents hangs.

## Interactive + Headless

The tour driver must work in BOTH:

- interactive mode (real renderer)
- headless mode (for CI regression tests)

Headless mode should be able to execute the tour and emit JSON markers that tests can snapshot (see `bd-bqd1`).

## Implementation Approach

- Introduce a TourRunner in the demo binary:
  - state: TourState { step_idx, step_started_at, paused, ... }
  - fn tick(now, app, out_actions)

- The tour runner should not directly draw; it only manipulates app state and injects actions.

- When the tour wants to show something, it should set a TourHighlight { rect, message } that the tour overlay bead renders.

## Acceptance Notes

- cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --tour produces a stable “demo story” without user input.
- Tour can be paused/resumed and cleanly exited.
- Tour never leaves the app in a broken focus/input state when it ends.
- With `--exit-after-tour`, the process terminates cleanly at completion.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- `cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --tour` produces a complete, deterministic feature walkthrough without user input.

 {"id":"bd-3ot0","title":"demo_showcase runtime: optional ThreadedRenderer backend (--threaded)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Optional ThreadedRenderer Backend (`--threaded`)

## Purpose

OpenTUI has a `ThreadedRenderer` that moves terminal I/O off the main thread. The flagship demo should be able to showcase this as an optional mode.

This is not required for the first “works and looks amazing” milestone, but it’s an impressive proof point for real applications.

## Behavior

- CLI flag: `--threaded`
- When enabled, the demo uses `opentui::renderer::ThreadedRenderer` instead of `Renderer`.
- The UI + state machine should behave the same.

## Design Constraint

`Renderer` and `ThreadedRenderer` have different ownership models (threaded moves buffers/pools through channels). To avoid duplicating the whole app:

- Introduce a small demo-only backend wrapper:
  - `enum Backend { Direct(Renderer), Threaded(ThreadedRenderer) }`
  - helper methods: `buffer()`, `present()`, `resize()`, `set_title()`, `set_cursor()`

## GraphemePool Note (Important)

If tour/unicode panels use grapheme pool content, ensure that the threaded path does not leak grapheme refs:

- Avoid using `ThreadedRenderer::clear()` if it doesn’t clear with pool.
- Prefer explicit `buffer().clear_with_pool(grapheme_pool(), bg)` / `resize_with_pool` patterns.
- If the library needs a small fix (pool-aware clear/resize convenience), file a follow-up bead or patch the library directly.

## Acceptance Notes

- `cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --threaded` works and exits cleanly.
- Rendering remains smooth and input remains responsive.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:

 {"id":"bd-3vjm","title":"demo_showcase runtime: auto-exit on tour completion (--exit-after-tour)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Auto‑Exit on Tour Completion (`--exit-after-tour`)

## Purpose

We need a deterministic way for an unattended tour run to terminate cleanly so we can:

- record demos reliably (tour ends -> app exits)
- run PTY E2E tests without brittle "send keys" logic

This bead implements the runtime semantics of exiting when the tour completes.

## Behavior

- Flag: `--exit-after-tour`
- When tour mode reaches the final step and completes it, the process exits 0 automatically.

Exit must be clean:

- restore cursor
- leave alt screen (if used)
- disable mouse (if enabled)

This flag does **not** change interactive controls (Esc still exits tour early).

## Relationship to `--max-frames`

- `--max-frames` is a generic hard frame budget used as a safety bound (tracked separately in `bd-3lio`).
- PTY tests should typically run with BOTH:
  - `--exit-after-tour` (intended terminator)
  - `--max-frames` (hang-proof safety bound)

## Notes

- Interactive users should not be surprised:
  - Only enable this flag when explicitly requested.
  - Consider showing a small status hint (e.g., “auto-exit after tour”) so it’s discoverable.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:

 {"id":"bd-j7g","title":"Terminal Capability Queries","description":"## Overview
Send actual capability queries to the terminal instead of just checking environment variables. This enables accurate feature detection for advanced terminal features.

## Scope
Query sequences to send (per spec Section 2.7):
- DA1 (Primary Device Attributes): `ESC[c`
- DA2 (Secondary Device Attributes): `ESC[>c`
- XTVERSION: `ESC[>0q`
- Pixel resolution: `ESC[14t`
- Kitty keyboard protocol: `ESC[?u`

Parse responses to detect:
- Terminal type and version
- True color support
- Kitty keyboard protocol support
- Synchronized output support
- Sixel graphics support
- SGR pixel mouse mode

## Implementation Details

### Query Sequences
```rust
// In src/terminal/queries.rs
pub const DA1_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[c";           // Primary device attributes
pub const DA2_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[>c";          // Secondary device attributes
pub const XTVERSION_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[>0q";   // Terminal version
pub const PIXEL_SIZE_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[14t";  // Window pixel size
pub const KITTY_KEYBOARD_QUERY: &str = "\x1b[?u"; // Kitty keyboard support
```

### Response Parser
```rust
impl Terminal {
    /// Send capability queries and collect responses.
    /// Uses timeout to avoid blocking if terminal doesn't respond.
    pub fn query_capabilities(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> io::Result<()>;

    /// Parse a terminal response sequence.
    fn parse_response(&mut self, response: &[u8]) -> Option<TerminalResponse>;

    /// Check for pending responses (non-blocking).
    pub fn poll_responses(&mut self) -> Vec<TerminalResponse>;
}

pub enum TerminalResponse {
    DeviceAttributes { 
        primary: bool,  // DA1 vs DA2
        params: Vec<u32>,
    },
    XtVersion { 
        name: String, 
        version: String,
    },
    PixelSize { 
        width: u16, 
        height: u16,
    },
    KittyKeyboard { 
        flags: u32,
    },
    Unknown(Vec<u8>),
}
```

### Response Format Parsing
```
DA1 response: ESC [ ? Ps ; Ps ; ... c
  - Parse params to detect features (e.g., 4 = sixel)
  
DA2 response: ESC [ > Pp ; Pv ; Pc c
  - Pp = terminal type, Pv = firmware version, Pc = ROM cartridge
  
XTVERSION response: ESC P > | text ST
  - text contains terminal name and version
  
Pixel size response: ESC [ 4 ; height ; width t
  - Window size in pixels

Kitty keyboard response: ESC [ ? flags u
  - flags indicates supported modes
```

### Capability Updates
Map responses to Capabilities struct updates:
- DA1 param 4 → sixel = true
- XTVERSION contains "kitty" → kitty_keyboard = true, kitty_graphics = true
- XTVERSION contains "foot"/"alacritty"/"wezterm" → rgb = true
- Pixel size response → store for sixel/image calculations

## Files to Create/Modify
- src/terminal/queries.rs - new file for query constants and response types (~100 lines)
- src/terminal/mod.rs - add query methods and response handling (~150 lines)
- src/terminal/capabilities.rs - update from parsed responses (~50 lines)

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (minimum 10 tests)
```rust
// Response parsing tests
#[test] fn test_parse_da1_response_basic()
#[test] fn test_parse_da1_response_with_sixel()
#[test] fn test_parse_da2_response()
#[test] fn test_parse_xtversion_kitty()
#[test] fn test_parse_xtversion_alacritty()
#[test] fn test_parse_pixel_size_response()
#[test] fn test_parse_kitty_keyboard_response()
#[test] fn test_parse_unknown_response()

// Query generation tests
#[test] fn test_query_sequences_correct()
#[test] fn test_query_all_sends_all_queries()

// Integration tests
#[test] fn test_capabilities_updated_from_da1()
#[test] fn test_timeout_returns_partial_results()
```

### E2E Test (tests/e2e/capability_queries.rs)
```rust
/// Interactive test that queries real terminal and logs results.
/// Run with: cargo test --test capability_e2e -- --nocapture
#[test]
fn e2e_query_real_terminal() {
    // Setup logging
    env_logger::init();
    
    let mut terminal = Terminal::new()?;
    
    log::info!("Sending capability queries...");
    terminal.query_capabilities(Duration::from_secs(2))?;
    
    for response in terminal.poll_responses() {
        log::info!("Response: {:?}", response);
    }
    
    log::info!("Final capabilities: {:?}", terminal.capabilities());
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 5 query sequences sent in correct format
- [ ] DA1 response parsed correctly, sixel detected
- [ ] DA2 response parsed correctly
- [ ] XTVERSION response extracts terminal name/version
- [ ] Pixel size response extracts dimensions
- [ ] Kitty keyboard response parsed
- [ ] Unknown responses don't crash (return Unknown variant)
- [ ] Timeout prevents hanging on non-responsive terminals
- [ ] Capabilities struct updated from parsed responses
- [ ] All 10+ unit tests pass
- [ ] E2E test logs all responses clearly","acceptance_criteria":"- [ ] Queries sent in correct format

-{"id":"bd-jfno","title":"demo_showcase tests: core logic unit suite (layout/config/palette/log models)","description":"# demo_showcase Tests — Core Logic “Unit” Suite (Headless Checks)

## Purpose

We want unit-level confidence for the demo’s core logic without requiring a real TTY.

Because most demo code lives in the binary, the most reliable approach is to expose deterministic headless “checks” and validate them from integration tests.

This bead adds tests that cover:

- CLI/config parsing (flag combinations)
- layout math invariants (no negative rects, no overflow, compact mode triggers)
- command palette scoring + selection behavior
- log model behavior (ring buffer, selection, hyperlink row formatting)
- hit ID mapping invariants (IDs stable, no overlap across ranges)

## Approach

Extend headless support with a deterministic sub-mode that runs specific checks and emits JSON:

- --headless-check <name>

Examples:

- --headless-check layout
- --headless-check palette
- --headless-check hitgrid

Each check should:

- run without a TTY
- produce structured JSON with:
  - inputs
  - outputs
  - key derived values
- exit 0 on pass, non-zero on failure

Integration tests then:

- run cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --headless-check <name> --headless-dump-json

+{"id":"bd-jfno","title":"demo_showcase tests: core logic unit suite (layout/config/palette/log models)","description":"# demo_showcase Tests — Core Logic “Unit” Suite (Headless Checks)

## Purpose

We want unit-level confidence for the demo’s core logic without requiring a real TTY.

Because most demo code lives in the binary, the most reliable approach is to expose deterministic headless “checks” and validate them from integration tests.

This bead adds tests that cover:

- CLI/config parsing (flag combinations)
- layout math invariants (no negative rects, no overflow, compact mode triggers)
- command palette scoring + selection behavior
- log model behavior (ring buffer, selection, hyperlink row formatting)
- hit ID mapping invariants (IDs stable, no overlap across ranges)

## Approach

Extend headless support with a deterministic sub-mode that runs specific checks and emits JSON:

- --headless-check <name>

Examples:

- --headless-check layout
- --headless-check palette
- --headless-check hitgrid

Each check should:

- run without a TTY
- produce structured JSON with:
  - inputs
  - outputs
  - key derived values
- exit 0 on pass, non-zero on failure

Integration tests then:

- run cargo run --bin demo_showcase -- --headless-check <name> --headless-dump-json
- snapshot the JSON with insta

## Logging

- Always print a short summary line to stdout.
- On failure, dump detailed context to stderr.
- When HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1, write JSON to target/test-artifacts.

## Acceptance Notes

- The suite catches regressions in layout/palette/ID mapping before they show up as UI glitches.
- Failures are easy to diagnose from logs and JSON snapshots.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:

- Integration tests run a set of checks (layout/config/palette/log model/hit ids) and snapshot the JSON with insta.
- Failures exit non-zero and print actionable diagnostics plus a reproduction command.
- When `HARNESS_ARTIFACTS=1`, JSON/stdout/stderr are written under `target/test-artifacts`.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:08.436032991Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T04:35:21.076540356Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-1w9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:58.873257268Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-2c6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:51:00.588365689Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-2el","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:56.997493692Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-2iv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:57.654657671Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-35rk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:59.919227325Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-383","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:08.447911428Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jfno","depends_on_id":"bd-3ii","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:50:58.207386414Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-jqv","title":"demo_showcase UI: tour overlay + spotlight (step HUD + highlight)","description":"# demo_showcase UI — Tour Overlay + Spotlight (Step HUD + Highlight)

## Purpose

Tour mode is a core “unattended demo” deliverable. Visually, it needs a HUD that makes the tour understandable:

- what step we’re on
- what feature is being demonstrated
- what the viewer should notice

And it needs a “spotlight” effect so the viewer’s eye goes to the right area.

## Visual Requirements

- A compact step HUD overlay (top-right or centered) with:
  - step title
  - step counter (e.g., `3/12`)
  - short callout text (“Notice the alpha blended glass overlay”) 
  - controls: `Next`, `Pause`, `Exit` (clickable)

- A spotlight/highlight effect:
  - dim the entire screen with a translucent tint
  - “punch out” or brighten the target rectangle (panel or UI element)
  - optionally add an animated outline/glow around the target

Implementation note: since we don’t have true compositing masks, approximate punch-out by drawing dim backdrop first, then redraw the target region at full opacity and add a border.

## Input

- Keyboard: `Space` next, `P` pause, `Esc` exit tour
- Mouse: clicking `Next/Pause/Exit` uses HitGrid

## Integration

- Tour overlay rendering must be driven by the tour script state (`bd-1gy`) and tour runtime driver.
- The overlay should not require the command palette or help overlay; it is its own UI layer.","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- Tour HUD shows step title, step counter, short callout text, and controls (Next/Pause/Exit).
- Spotlight effect clearly directs attention: backdrop dim + re-render/outline of the highlighted target region.
- Tour overlay controls work via both keyboard and clickable HitGrid targets.
- Rendering behaves correctly in compact layout and small terminals (scroll/clipping as needed).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:24:55.998651790Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-27T23:19:07.165182203Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","ui"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-jqv","depends_on_id":"bd-1al","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:07.895805744Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jqv","depends_on_id":"bd-1gy","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:08.433392382Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jqv","depends_on_id":"bd-3o0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:32:08.966407429Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-jqv","depends_on_id":"bd-c75","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:24:56.010957522Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-pnln","title":"opentui API: expose terminal capabilities from Renderer (for inspector/gating)","description":"# OpenTUI API — Expose Terminal Capabilities from Renderer

## Purpose

The demo_showcase (and real applications) need to make capability decisions and display capability state:

- true color vs 256-color
- synchronized output support
- hyperlink support
- mouse / focus / bracketed paste availability

Today, `Terminal` exposes `capabilities()`, but `Renderer` does not.

This bead adds a small, ergonomic API surface so applications can query what the renderer believes about the terminal.

## Proposed API

- Renderer::capabilities(&self) -> &Capabilities

Optional (only if needed):

- Renderer::capabilities_mut(&mut self) -> &mut Capabilities (generally avoid unless the demo needs manual overrides)

## Notes

- Capabilities are currently populated by `Capabilities::detect()` plus any query responses that the terminal parser applies.
- Even if query responses are not fully wired in the renderer loop, exposing the detected capabilities is still valuable.

## Acceptance Notes

- demo_showcase inspector panel can display capability flags without re-detecting.
- No behavioral change for existing code (this is a pure accessor).","acceptance_criteria":"Done when:
- `Renderer` exposes a read-only accessor `capabilities(&self) -> &Capabilities`.
- demo_showcase (inspector + gating) can query capabilities without re-detecting.
- Existing behavior is unchanged (pure accessor) and the crate still passes clippy/tests.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:54:53.616746157Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T02:32:36.396265818Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T02:32:36.396200186Z","close_reason":"Added Renderer::capabilities() and capabilities_mut() methods to expose terminal capabilities. Also exported Capabilities and ColorSupport from crate root.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["api","demo_showcase"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-pnln","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:54:53.629301807Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-po1","title":"demo_showcase runtime: render pass orchestration (z-order, scissor/opacity conventions)","description":"# demo_showcase Runtime — Render Pass Orchestration

## Purpose

Define a consistent render pipeline so the demo has a stable “z-order” and the code remains understandable.

## Proposed Pass Order (Back-to-Front)

1. **Background**
   - Fill screen with `BG0`
   - Subtle gradient noise/pulse (low alpha)

2. **Chrome**
   - Top bar (gradient header)
   - Status bar (key hints + stats)

3. **Panels** (each clipped)
   - Sidebar
   - Editor
   - Preview
   - Logs

4. **Overlays** (semi-transparent, “glass”)
   - Help
   - Command palette
   - Tour overlay

5. **Toasts**
   - Small ephemeral notifications stacked in a corner

6. **Debug / Perf Overlay**
   - OpenTUI’s `Renderer::set_debug_overlay(true)` is separate, but we can also draw our own lightweight stats.

## Scissor + Opacity Conventions

- Every panel uses:
  - `push_scissor(inner_rect)` before content
  - optional `push_opacity(alpha)` for “glass” content areas
  - then pop in reverse order

- Overlays:
  - full-screen scissor
  - first draw dimming rectangle with opacity
  - then draw modal panel

## Grapheme Pool Conventions

- Default UI text can use `buffer.draw_text`.
- Any place we want to **prove multi-codepoint graphemes** must use pool-aware drawing:
  - `let (buf, pool) = renderer.buffer_with_pool();`
  - `buf.draw_text_with_pool(pool, ...)` or `TextBufferView::render_to_with_pool(...)`

## HitGrid Conventions

- `renderer.clear()` already clears the hit grid.
- While rendering, register hit areas *after* layout is computed.
- For nested hit areas, use `push_hit_scissor()` matching the visual scissor so clicks align with clipped visuals.","acceptance_criteria":"Checklist:
- [ ] Render pass order is implemented and documented in code
- [ ] Scissor/opacity usage follows a consistent convention
- [ ] Pool-aware rendering is used intentionally where needed
- [ ] HitGrid registration aligns with visual layout + clipping","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:10:19.480963690Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T00:40:35.588396204Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T00:40:35.588266863Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["demo_showcase","rendering","runtime"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-po1","depends_on_id":"bd-1ok","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:12:17.874683866Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-po1","depends_on_id":"bd-29z","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:10:19.504111190Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-po1","depends_on_id":"bd-35g","type":"related","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:46:49.843230976Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-po1","depends_on_id":"bd-3ii","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T22:12:15.346407203Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
